quoteFirst: It was fifteen minutes of midnight when I arrived home, let myself in with my latch key which I have been carrying as a silent reminder of my former terrifically wild (?) career; routed out the folks, and sat swathed in bath-robe and dressing-gown until
quoteLast: , just talking. It was bully. And then I tumbled into my own bed and slept and slept and slept.
title: Conscript 2989: Experiences of a Drafted Man
author: Crump, Irving, 1887-1979; Martin, H. B. [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: I inquired the way to an inn, of a little rascal with hair over his eyes, who cocked up his little spaniel's nose to look at me at his ease; I gave him a few sous for his trouble, and a conscientious rap with my crop which sent him away squeaking like a jay plucked alive. I threw myself on a bed and fell into a sound sleep. When I awoke, it was
quoteLast: in the afternoon, and even that was hardly enough to rest me completely. Indeed, it was none too much for a sleepless night, an intrigue, a duel, and a very rapid, although triumphant, flight.
title: Mademoiselle de Maupin, Volume 2 (of 2)
author: Gautier, Théophile, 1811-1872; Le Sueur, François-Xavier [Illustrator]; Toudouze, Édouard, 1848-1907 [Illustrator]; Burnham, I. G. [Translator]

quoteFirst: In our two daily classes, one in the morning from eight o’clock to half-past nine, the other in the afternoon from
quoteLast: until half-past four, I had a great deal of trouble to fix the attention of the little idler. Seated on his chair, opposite me on the other side of the table, and rolling his tongue against his cheek, while he covered the paper with his big awkward writing, he would now and then glance up at me.
title: The disciple
author: Bourget, Paul, 1852-1935

quoteLast: on Thursday the convoy is ready.  Thirty bullocks have been harnessed to _Long Tom_.  The rest of the convoy consists of some twenty waggons of provisions and ammunition.
title: Ten Months in the Field with the Boers
author: Anonymous

quoteLast: , game or no game, we will turn our faces homeward again.”
title: Tour of the Zero Club

quoteFirst: The next day was what was called "a jubilee-masquerade in the Venetian manner" at Ranelagh: it had nothing Venetian in it, but was by far the best understood and the prettiest spectacle I ever saw: nothing in a fairy tale ever surpassed it. One of the proprietors, who is a German, and belongs to court, had got my Lady Yarmouth to persuade the King to order it.  It began
quoteLast: , and, about five, people of fashion began to go.  When you entered, you found the whole garden filled with masks and spread with tents, which remained all night very commodely.
title: The Letters of Horace Walpole, Earl of Orford — Volume 2
author: Walpole, Horace, 1717-1797

quoteFirst: I looked away hastily, and, I am ashamed to add, I trembled. Frenchy made no appearance at lunch, but towards
quoteLast: of the afternoon I saw him steal stealthily out and get a bottle of whisky and some biscuits, and then close his door again on our little world. I was struck afresh with his gross, evil look, and shrank, as one might from a wild beast, at the very sight of him.
title: The Queen Versus Billy, and Other Stories
author: Osbourne, Lloyd, 1868-1947

title: The adventures of Heine
author: Wallace, Edgar, 1875-1932

quoteFirst: It was an early August morning, between two and
quoteLast: . The only doctor in the village had been out all night at a little town about five miles away. He was wanted at once, and I volunteered to get him.
